1: Shadow / Type. Fire from heaven descends and consumes the burnt offering — the divine acceptance of Solomon’s sacrifice confirmed by supernatural fire, exactly as when Aaron’s first sacrifice was accepted (Leviticus 9:24). The fire is the Father’s “yes” to the offering. At the cross, the Father accepted Christ’s self-offering — the resurrection is the divine fire of vindication that consumed and transformed what was offered. At Pentecost, “tongues as of fire” (Acts 2:3) rest on the believers who are the new temple, signaling that the Spirit-fire of divine acceptance now dwells in them rather than on an altar.
2: Shadow. The priests cannot enter the house because the glory of YHWH fills it — the overwhelming presence excludes even the dedicated ministers. Contrast with the new covenant: “Therefore brothers, since we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus... let us draw near” (Hebrews 10:19-22). The glory that excluded the priests from Solomon’s temple now, through Christ’s blood, becomes the glory that welcomes every believer into the divine presence. The exclusion of 7:2 is answered by the invitation of Hebrews 10:22.
3: Theme. All Israel bows face to the pavement and worships — the prostrate posture of creatures before the Creator, spontaneously enacted when the divine glory falls. In Revelation, the elders and living creatures prostrate themselves before the Lamb “slain from the foundation of the world” (Revelation 5:6-14) — the same posture now directed toward Christ as the worthy one who purchased people for God with his blood. The “his steadfast love endures forever” refrain (Ps 136) becomes in Revelation 5:9 the new song of the redeemed from every tribe and tongue and people.
4: Shadow. The king and all the people offer sacrifices before YHWH. The repeated offerings of the Solomonic era — countless animals across the temple’s history — attest that the sacrificial system pointed beyond itself. Hebrews 10:1-4 states the logic directly: “it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins.” Every animal offered at Solomon’s altar was a promissory note pointing to the one perfect offering that would actually accomplish what the system shadowed.
5: Shadow. Twenty-two thousand bulls and one hundred twenty thousand sheep — the enormous scale of Solomon’s dedication offering. The very scale signals the inadequacy of animal blood: no quantity of animals can exhaust the requirement. Hebrews 10:10-14 resolves the insufficiency: “by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified.” Where Solomon needed thousands upon thousands, Christ needed only once — and once was infinitely sufficient.
6: Theme. The priests and Levites with musical instruments praising YHWH “for his steadfast love endures forever” — the refrain from Pss 118 and 136. Christ’s resurrection is the ultimate demonstration that “his steadfast love endures forever” — his love could not be extinguished by death or held by the grave. The eternal worship of the redeemed (Revelation 5:9-14; 2 Chronicles 15:3-4) fulfills this liturgical pattern; the “new song” they sing has the same theme — the unfailing covenant love of God, now revealed most fully in the Lamb.
7: Shadow. Solomon consecrates the middle of the court as an altar space because the bronze altar was too small for the burnt offerings. The existing structure was insufficient for the scale of what was offered. This shadows the cross: the entire temple complex with all its altars was insufficient for the weight of human sin. The true offering required something beyond all temple altars — “he suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood” (Hebrews 13:12), outside the sacred precincts, on a different kind of altar altogether.
8: Shadow. The feast of seven days followed by another seven days — the Feast of Tabernacles combined with the temple dedication. The Feast of Tabernacles was the great harvest festival celebrating YHWH’s provision in the wilderness and the ingathering of the harvest. John 7:37-39 places Jesus’ “rivers of living water” declaration at the climax of the Feast of Tabernacles, directly claiming to be the fulfillment of what the feast anticipated. Christ is the ultimate tabernacle — “the Word became flesh and tabernacled (ĕskēnōsen) among us” (John 1:14).
9: Shadow. The eighth day — the solemn assembly after the seven days of feasting. The eighth day of the tabernacles celebration carries eschatological significance: after the seven-day week of this age comes the eighth day, the day of new creation. The resurrection of Jesus happened on the first day of the week — also the “eighth day” after the previous week — inaugurating the new creation. The “solemn assembly” (ʿăṣeret) of the eighth day prefigures the gathered assembly of the redeemed in the age to come.
10: Theme. The people returned home joyful and glad of heart for the good that YHWH had done for David and Solomon and Israel his people. The joy of completion — the temple is built, the glory has fallen, the offerings are accepted. The fuller joy is the “joy set before Christ” for which he endured the cross (Hebrews 12:2), and the joy of his people who receive “an inheritance that is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading” (1 Peter 1:4) — a joy that flows from a work more final than Solomon’s.
11: Type. “Thus Solomon finished the house of YHWH and the king’s own house.” The completion of both the sacred and the royal project in one reign. Christ’s “It is finished” (John 19:30, tetelestai) is the definitive completion — the work of salvation, the true temple-building project, accomplished once for all. Solomon’s completion was provisional (the temple would be destroyed in 586 BCE); Christ’s completion is eternal and indestructible.
12: Theme. “I have heard your prayer and have chosen this place for myself as a house of sacrifice.” The Father’s answer to prayer: heard and chosen. The Father’s answer to Christ’s prayer (John 11:42, “Father, I thank you that you have heard me”; John 17:24, “Father, I desire that they also... may be with me where I am”) is the resurrection and the glorification — the Father always hears the Son. This verse assures every petitioner that the God who heard Solomon hears Christ, and through Christ hears his people.
13: Theme. “When I shut up the heavens so that there is no rain, or command the locust to devour the land, or send pestilence among my people” — divine sovereignty over natural disaster. Christ who stilled the storm (Mark 4:39), healed the sick (Mark 1:34), and cast out demons (Mark 1:34) demonstrated in his ministry that the sovereign Lord of creation had entered creation to reverse curse. His resurrection is the beginning of the reversal of the entire order of decay and death (Romans 8:21-22).
14: Theme. “If my people who are called by my name humble themselves, and pray and seek my face and turn from their wicked ways, then I will hear from heaven and will forgive their sin and heal their land.” The great conditional promise. Christ fulfills every condition on behalf of his people: he humbled himself to the point of death on a cross (Philippians 2:8), he prays perfectly (Hebrews 7:25), he sought the Father’s face (John 17), he is the one without sin who turned entirely from wickedness (2 Corinthians 5:21). In Christ, all conditions are perfectly met — the healing of the land (Romans 8:21; Revelation 21:5) flows from his completed obedience (2 Corinthians 1:20).
15: Theme. “Now my eyes will be open and my ears attentive to the prayer offered in this place.” The permanent divine attentiveness to the temple. Christ is the permanent open heaven: his ascension inaugurated the continuous intercession that keeps the heavenly sanctuary open to his people (Hebrews 7:25; 2 Chronicles 9:24). “The eyes of the Lord are toward the righteous and his ears toward their cry” (Psalms 34:15; 1 Peter 3:12) — permanently true for those who are righteous in Christ.
16: Theme. “I have chosen and consecrated this house that my name may be there forever. My eyes and my heart will be there for all time.” The divine consecration of the temple as permanent — God’s name, eyes, and heart pledged to it. Christ is the one on whom the divine name is permanently placed (Revelation 3:12; 2 Chronicles 14:1), and the one to whom the Father’s heart is permanently turned (“You are my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased,” Matthew 3:17). His people, named with his name, inherit this divine attention — “the Lord knows those who are his” (2 Timothy 2:19).
17: Theme. “And as for you, if you walk before me as David your father walked…” — the conditional covenant: perfect obedience required. Christ is the one person in all of history who perfectly walked before God — “I always do what pleases the Father” (John 8:29). Where Solomon and every subsequent son of David failed this condition, Christ fulfills it perfectly, securing the covenant promises not through conditional human obedience that might fail but through his own perfect and permanent obedience (Romans 5:19).
18: Direct. “There shall not fail you a man to sit on the throne of Israel.” Acts 2:30-31 applies this directly to Christ: David “being therefore a prophet… he foresaw and spoke about the resurrection of the Christ.” Acts 13:34 adds: “that he raised him from the dead, no more to return to corruption, he has spoken in this way: ‘I will give you the holy and sure blessings of David.’” The resurrection guarantees that the Davidic throne is permanently and indestructibly occupied — Christ reigns and cannot be dethroned.
19: Shadow. “But if you turn aside and forsake my statutes and my commandments that I have set before you.” The conditional curse — faithlessness leads to exile and disgrace. This threat was literally fulfilled in 586 BCE. But Christ underwent the fulfilment of the covenant curses in his own person: he was “cut off from the land of the living” (Isaiah 53:8), abandoned, cast out of the holy city, crucified outside the gate (Hebrews 13:12). He absorbed the curse-of-exile so that his people would receive the blessing-of-return.
20: Shadow. “Then I will pluck them up from my land that I have given them” — the ultimate covenant threat: removal from the land. Christ was “plucked up” — taken from among the living — so that his people would never be permanently removed from God’s presence. His promise stands: “I will never leave you nor forsake you” (Hebrews 13:5); “no one will snatch them out of my hand” (John 10:28). The covenant exile that Solomon’s warning threatened is answered by the one who went into exile in their place.
21: Shadow. “And this house, which was exalted, everyone passing by will be astonished and say, ‘Why has YHWH done this?’” The desolated temple becomes an object lesson for the nations. The cross is the greater desolation that becomes the greater object lesson: Christ hanging outside the city gate, the one through whom all things were made, destroyed in a public spectacle. The answer to “Why has God done this?” is 2 Corinthians 5:21: “For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin.” The desolation of the Lamb answers the desolation of the temple.
22: Theme. “Because they abandoned YHWH the God of their fathers who brought them out of Egypt, and laid hold on other gods and worshipped them and served them.” The covenant apostasy verdict — the explanation for ruin. Christ was abandoned (“My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” — Matthew 27:46) not for his own apostasy (he had none) but for ours. He entered the place of God-abandonment that covenant sin produces, so that we who had abandoned God might be received back. The terminus of the covenant curse is the cross; the terminus of the curse on the cross is the resurrection.
